document_metadata.common_name: Georgia Declaration of Causes of Secession (1861)
document_metadata.analyst_prior_note: Pool asymmetry disclosed in methodology_v0.4.md. The Georgia Declaration is unique in the corpus in that its stated purpose is explicitly persuasive: it presents causes to 'confederates and the world.' It is not a law or policy instrument but a political manifesto. This affects the epistemic character of its claims: many are advocacy assertions designed to persuade, but they contain verifiable factual propositions (historical events, legal instruments, organizational characterizations) that can be evaluated. The central bridge_narrative candidate is the 'constitutional rights defense' framing: the Declaration positions secession as a response to Northern violations of constitutional obligations, while explicitly and repeatedly identifying the defense of slavery and slaveholder property as the actual cause. This is not a case where the bridge narrative conceals the cause from the document's authors; they state it openly. The bridge narrative operates at the level of legitimacy: 'constitutional rights' provides a frame that is more politically defensible in a world audience than 'defense of the right to enslave human beings.' P-003 (Republican party intent to abolish slavery in existing states) is anchored_false: Lincoln's stated position was non-extension to territories, explicitly not abolition where it existed. P-002 (Northern non-compliance with fugitive slave clause) is anchored_true as a description of Northern personal liberty laws, but contextually_misleading as a constitutional grievance that justifies dissolution of the Union. P-001 ('subordination of the African race was fully conceded by all') is anchored_false: abolitionists, free Black communities, and significant portions of the Northern population had explicitly contested this claim for decades. Note: wavelet dual-band scoring not available this extraction pass; band assignments are analyst-derived.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Georgia Secession Declaration was approved on January 29, 1861. Georgia had voted to secede on January 19, 1861. The Declaration was written to accompany and explain the secession ordinance. It explicitly states that its purpose is to 'present to their confederates and the world the causes which have led to the separation.' The document explicitly and repeatedly identifies African slavery and slaveholder property rights as the central subject of the dispute. The word 'slavery' or 'slave' appears in the document more than 30 times.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Georgia Secession Ordinance, January 19, 1861 (Georgia state records); Declaration text (Avalon Project) — self-evidencing as to content and stated purpose. Charles Dew, Apostles of Disunion: Southern Secession Commissioners and the Causes of the Civil War (University Press of Virginia, 2001) — independent scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: The Declaration text is self-evidencing as to its stated content and purpose. Dew (2001) is independent academic scholarship entirely outside the governance chain of the Georgia secession convention.

anchor_registry.notes: Foundational anchor. Establishes the document's character as explicit advocacy with stated purpose, and its explicit identification of slavery as the central cause, against which all legitimacy framings must be evaluated.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: Lincoln's First Inaugural Address (March 4, 1861) explicitly stated: 'I have no purpose, directly or indirectly, to interfere with the institution of slavery in the States where it exists. I believe I have no lawful right to do so, and I have no inclination to do so.' Lincoln's October 16, 1854 Peoria Address and his 1860 campaign positions consistently held that the Republican party's position was non-extension of slavery to new territories, not abolition in existing slave states. The Republican Party platform of 1860 explicitly denied any intention to interfere with slavery in states where it existed. Lincoln received less than 40% of the popular vote in 1860 and appeared on no ballot in ten Southern states, demonstrating that the Republican Party could not command a Congressional majority sufficient to pass any slavery-abolition legislation.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Lincoln's First Inaugural Address, March 4, 1861 (Public Papers of the Presidents, National Archives) — self-evidencing. Republican Party Platform of 1860 (available in Kirk Porter and Donald Johnson, eds., National Party Platforms, University of Illinois Press, 1966) — independent compilation. 1860 election results documented in Walter Dean Burnham, Presidential Ballots 1836-1892 (Johns Hopkins University Press, 1955) — independent scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Lincoln's addresses are public government records self-evidencing as to their content. The Republican platform and election results are public institutional records independent of the Georgia secession convention governance chain.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-003 (Republican party intent to abolish slavery in existing states). Lincoln's explicit statements are the direct falsifying evidence.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The abolitionist movement in the United States had been active and vocal since the 1830s. William Lloyd Garrison published The Liberator beginning January 1, 1831. The American Anti-Slavery Society was founded in 1833. Free Black intellectuals including Frederick Douglass (who escaped slavery in 1838 and published his Narrative in 1845), Sojourner Truth, and David Walker (whose Appeal to the Coloured Citizens of the World, 1829, explicitly challenged claims of racial subordination) publicly and explicitly contested the claim that racial subordination was 'conceded by all.' The 1860 population of free Black Americans was approximately 488,000. Anti-slavery sentiment among Northern white citizens had been growing since the 1840s through the Free Soil movement, the antislavery Whigs, and eventually the Republican Party.
anchor_registry.classification_source: David Walker's Appeal to the Coloured Citizens of the World, 1829 (published text, self-evidencing); Frederick Douglass, Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave, 1845 (self-evidencing); Eighth US Census (1860) documenting free Black population; Henry Mayer, All on Fire: William Lloyd Garrison and the Abolition of Slavery (St. Martin's Press, 1998) — independent scholarship.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Walker's Appeal and Douglass's Narrative are primary source texts entirely independent of the Georgia secession governance chain. The 1860 Census is a federal government record independent of the Georgia state convention. Mayer (1998) is independent academic scholarship.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-001 ('subordination of the African race was fully conceded by all'). The documented abolitionist movement and free Black intellectual and political activism directly falsify the 'universally conceded' claim.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: The Personal Liberty Laws enacted by Northern states were laws that restricted state assistance to the enforcement of the Fugitive Slave Acts of 1793 and 1850, prohibited use of state jails for holding alleged fugitives, and in some cases provided legal counsel for persons claimed as fugitives. By 1861, approximately 14 Northern states had enacted personal liberty laws of varying scope. In Prigg v. Pennsylvania (1842), the Supreme Court had held that states were not required to actively assist in enforcement of the federal fugitive slave law but could not obstruct it. The Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 was unpopular in the North and was widely resisted by individuals and communities, but it remained in force as federal law through 1864.
anchor_registry.classification_source: Prigg v. Pennsylvania, 41 U.S. 539 (1842) — Supreme Court decision, self-evidencing as to holding. Thomas Morris, Free Men All: The Personal Liberty Laws of the North 1780-1861 (Johns Hopkins University Press, 1974) — independent scholarship. Fugitive Slave Act of 1850, 9 Stat. 462 — public law, self-evidencing.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Prigg v. Pennsylvania is a Supreme Court decision self-evidencing as to its holding. Morris (1974) is independent academic scholarship. The Fugitive Slave Act is a public law self-evidencing as to its content.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-002 (Northern non-compliance with fugitive slave constitutional obligations). Personal liberty laws existed and represented genuine Northern resistance to federal fugitive slave enforcement, but the constitutional-obligation framing requires contextual evaluation against Prigg and the continued federal law status.

anchor_registry.anchor_text: Dred Scott v. Sandford (60 U.S. 393, 1857) held that enslaved persons were property, not citizens, that Congress lacked authority to prohibit slavery in territories, and that the Missouri Compromise's restrictions were unconstitutional. This ruling was explicitly cited by proslavery advocates as confirming slaveholder property rights. The Confederacy's Constitution (March 11, 1861) explicitly protected slavery by name in Article I, Section 9(4) ('No bill of attainder, ex-post-facto law, or law denying or impairing the right of property in negro slaves shall be passed') and required new states to protect slavery in Article IV, Section 3(3). Confederate Vice President Alexander Stephens' Cornerstone Speech (March 21, 1861) stated explicitly that slavery was 'the cornerstone' of the Confederate government and 'the proper status of the negro in our form of civilization.'
anchor_registry.classification_source: Dred Scott v. Sandford, 60 U.S. 393 (1857) — Supreme Court decision, self-evidencing. Confederate Constitution, March 11, 1861 (Avalon Project) — public document, self-evidencing. Alexander Stephens, Cornerstone Speech, March 21, 1861 (Savannah Republican, March 22, 1861) — contemporaneous press record.

independent_genesis.independent_source_citation: Dred Scott is a Supreme Court decision self-evidencing as to its holding. The Confederate Constitution is a public document self-evidencing as to its provisions. Stephens' speech is documented in contemporaneous press independent of the Georgia secession convention.

anchor_registry.notes: Evaluates P-004 (constitutional protection of slaveholder property) and P-006-rel (bridge_narrative). The Confederate Constitution's explicit slavery provisions and Stephens' Cornerstone Speech provide the clearest subsequent evidence of what the secession was actually designed to protect, confirming the bridge_narrative analysis.

proposition_table.source_location: Body paragraph 4 (discussion of constitutional formation and slavery)
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: The subordination and the political and social inequality of the African race was fully conceded by all [at the time of the Constitution's formation].
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The subordination and the political and social inequality of the African race was fully conceded by all [at the time of the Constitution's formation].

proposition_table.source_location: Opening paragraph and body (fugitive slave clause discussion)
proposition_table.contextual_qualification_note: Anchored_true for the factual predicate of Northern resistance. The legal inference — that this constitutes a constitutional obligation justifying secession — is normative_stable (deeply contested; Lincoln's position was that secession itself was the constitutional violation) and is evaluated separately in P-005.
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: The non-slave-holding States have persistently refused to comply with their express constitutional obligations to us in reference to [enslaved persons/property], specifically with respect to the fugitive slave clause.
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The non-slave-holding States have persistently refused to comply with their express constitutional obligations to us in reference to [enslaved persons/property], specifically with respect to the fugitive slave clause.

proposition_table.source_location: Body paragraphs (discussion of Republican party and its purpose)
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: The Republican party and its incoming administration have declared a purpose to limit, restrain, and finally abolish slavery in the States where it exists; their prohibition of slavery everywhere is their cardinal principle.
proposition_table.normalized_claim: The Republican party and its incoming administration have declared a purpose to limit, restrain, and finally abolish slavery in the States where it exists; their prohibition of slavery everywhere is their cardinal principle.

proposition_table.source_location: Multiple body paragraphs (property rights framing throughout)
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: Slaveholders have constitutionally protected property rights in enslaved persons, and these rights are protected under the Constitution against federal deprivation.
proposition_table.normalized_claim: Slaveholders have constitutionally protected property rights in enslaved persons, and these rights are protected under the Constitution against federal deprivation.

proposition_table.source_location: Final paragraph ('To avoid these evils we resume the powers which our fathers delegated to the Government')
proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: Georgia's separation from the United States is constitutionally justified as a resumption of the powers delegated to the federal government.
proposition_table.normalized_claim: Georgia's separation from the United States is constitutionally justified as a resumption of the powers delegated to the federal government.

proposition_table.verbatim_source_text: [Relational] The 'constitutional rights' and 'constitutional obligations' framing positions secession as a principled legal response to Northern constitutional violations, insulating the explicit purpose of defending enslaved-person property rights from evaluation on its merits as a cause of dissolution of republican government.
proposition_table.normalized_claim: [Relational] The 'constitutional rights' and 'constitutional obligations' framing positions secession as a principled legal response to Northern constitutional violations, insulating the explicit purpose of defending enslaved-person property rights from evaluation on its merits as a cause of dissolution of republican government.

relational_resolution.relational_note: 'Constitutional rights' and 'constitutional obligations' framing creates a legitimacy frame that diverts scrutiny from the explicit slaveholder property defense as the stated cause of secession

confidence_flags.ambiguity_description: The Georgia Declaration is the clearest case of a bridge_narrative in the corpus because the document explicitly states the cause it seeks to legitimize (slavery and slaveholder property) while simultaneously providing a constitutional-rights framing intended to present that cause favorably to 'the world.' The bridge is high severity because: (1) P-001 (anchored_false) provides a false empirical foundation for the declaration's claims about racial order; (2) P-003 (anchored_false) overstates the Republican threat to existing slavery, making the constitutional defense framing appear proportionate; (3) P-002 (anchored_true: Northern personal liberty laws existed) provides genuine grounding for the constitutional grievance, enabling the false predicates P-001 and P-003 to leverage a true one. This three-predicate structure — true grievance + false racial premise + false threat characterization → constitutional rights legitimacy frame — is a textbook bridge_narrative architecture. ANC-005 (Stephens' Cornerstone Speech) provides the explicit post-Declaration admission that slavery was the foundational organizing principle.

The Georgia Declaration is explicitly advocacy rather than a legislative or executive act. Its claims are intended to persuade rather than to describe. This genre difference affects the epistemic weight of the anchored_false findings: the false claims (P-001, P-003) may reflect strategic advocacy choices rather than genuine belief on the part of the document's authors. However, the RGA methodology evaluates the epistemic status of claims independent of authorial intent; a false claim in a persuasive document still occupies a false position in the geometric space. The advocacy genre may affect the stress-energy contribution (a persuasive document's false claims may exert different gravitational pull on the discourse than a law's false claims). Pipeline should note the document genre when computing T_μν.
